text.title: ops-financial-basics Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for ops-financial-basics as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Calculating LTV and CAC but ignoring gross margin. A company with ARPU $100, churn 5%, and CAC $50 appears to have LTV:CAC equals 2x, but if gross margin is only 40%, the true margin per customer is $40, not $100.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Gross margin is essential because it represents the cash available to cover the acquisition cost and future expenses. A high ARPU with low margin is fragile; a lower ARPU with high margin is more resilient.

failure-mode.description: Including non-cash expenses (depreciation, stock options, accrued vacation) in the P and L without tracking cash burn separately.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: A founder can be "profitable on paper" while burning cash. Track cash-basis P and L (cash in / cash out) separately from accrual-basis for decision-making.

failure-mode.description: Acquiring customers aggressively while gross margin is 55 percent, assuming "we'll optimize later."
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: "Later" does not come. Every new customer at 55 percent margin accelerates cash burn. Investors and co-founders will pressure for profitability, not for optimization. Fix margin before scaling.

failure-mode.description: Offering unlimited free customer support or custom integrations for every customer, disguising labor costs in "customer success" instead of COGS.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Hidden labor costs erode margin in invisible ways. If 20 customers require 5 hours of custom integration each (100 hours), at $100/hour contractor cost, that is $10k/month in hidden COGS. Recognize it, price for it, or eliminate the service.

failure-mode.description: Enabling every team member to subscribe to any tool they request, resulting in $500 plus monthly in duplicate or unused subscriptions.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Tool sprawl is insidious because each subscription feels small until they aggregate. A CEO using Slack, HubSpot, Notion, Zapier, Intercom, and Calendly may have 6 subscriptions times $100-500 each equals $2k plus monthly with no visibility. Require procurement approval for tools greater than $50/month.
